name: Calliope
description: Calliope, whose name translates to "beautiful-voiced," is the eldest of the nine Muses in Greek mythology, daughters of Zeus, king of the gods, and Mnemosyne, the Titan goddess of memory. She presides over eloquence and epic poetry, inspiring poets and orators with her divine grace. As the Muse of epic poetry, she is often depicted holding a writing tablet or scroll and a stylus, symbols of her association with the written word. Calliope is considered the most esteemed of the Muses, possessing a regal bearing and profound wisdom. Her role extends beyond mere inspiration; she represents the power of language to shape understanding, preserve history, and immortalize heroes. She is also occasionally associated with rhetoric and science, reflecting the breadth of knowledge encompassed by the Muses' patronage. Her influence is evident in the invocations of epic poets like Homer and Virgil, who sought her guidance in crafting their monumental works. Calliope embodies the enduring importance of art and knowledge in shaping human civilization.
subtitle: Muse of Epic Poetry

keyMyths.title: Judgment of Persephone
keyMyths.description: Calliope was chosen to arbitrate the dispute between Aphrodite and Persephone over the possession of Adonis. Her judgment, reflecting wisdom and impartiality, decreed that Adonis should spend a portion of the year with each goddess. This myth showcases Calliope's role as a dispenser of justice and her ability to navigate complex conflicts with fairness and insight, highlighting her profound understanding of both love and loss.

keyMyths.title: Mother of Orpheus
keyMyths.description: Calliope is most famously known as the mother of Orpheus, the legendary musician and poet, fathered by either the god Apollo or the Thracian king Oeagrus. She bestowed upon Orpheus his extraordinary musical talent, which allowed him to charm all living things with his lyre. This connection highlights Calliope's power to inspire and transmit artistic ability, solidifying her role as the source of profound creativity and musical genius.

keyMyths.title: The Daughters of Pierus
keyMyths.description: The Pierides, daughters of Pierus, challenged the Muses to a musical contest. Calliope, representing the Muses, defeated them with her divine song. As punishment for their hubris, the Pierides were transformed into magpies, forever chattering and mimicking sounds without true understanding. This myth emphasizes the superiority of divine inspiration over mortal ambition and highlights the consequences of challenging the established order of the gods.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Provides an early account of Calliope as one of the nine Muses, daughters of Zeus and Mnemosyne.
sources.text: Provides an early account of Calliope as one of the nine Muses, daughters of Zeus and Mnemosyne.

sources.author: Pseudo-Apollodorus
sources.description: Details various myths involving Calliope, including her role in the judgment of Adonis.
sources.text: Details various myths involving Calliope, including her role in the judgment of Adonis.

sources.author: Ovid
sources.description: Presents the myth of Orpheus and Calliope, and the contest with the Pierides, emphasizing Calliope's musical prowess and divine authority.
sources.text: Presents the myth of Orpheus and Calliope, and the contest with the Pierides, emphasizing Calliope's musical prowess and divine authority.
